text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
CT CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

EXAMINATION: Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast, and concurrent computed tomography of the chest with intravenous contrast, performed as staging examination.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 47-year-old female with newly identified bilateral complex adnexal masses on pelvic ultrasound, large-volume ascites, elevated serum CA-125 (4,872 U/mL), and markedly elevated HE4. Referred for staging evaluation by gynecologic oncology. Rule out malignancy, assess extent of disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multi-detector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed using a multi-phase protocol with 90 mL of Omnipaque 350 iohexol administered intravenously via right antecubital vein at 3 mL/sec. Imaging was performed in the portal venous phase (70-second delay) for the abdomen and pelvis, with an additional delayed phase through the pelvis to optimize evaluation of adnexal structures. CT chest performed in the pulmonary arterial phase. Oral contrast (dilute Gastrografin) administered 45 minutes prior to examination. Axial reconstructions at 3 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats reviewed. No prior imaging available for comparison.

---

FINDINGS

CT CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The visualized lung parenchyma demonstrates no discrete pulmonary nodules, no masses, and no consolidative opacities. Lung windows are clear bilaterally. The trachea and central bronchi are patent and midline. No endobronchial lesions identified.

Pleurae: No pleural effusion identified bilaterally. No pleural thickening or pleural-based nodularity.

Mediastinum: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. No anterior mediastinal mass. The thymus is involuted, appropriate for age.

Vessels: The aorta and major mediastinal vessels are of normal caliber without aneurysm or dissection.

Chest wall and bony thorax: No suspicious osseous lesions. No lytic or sclerotic foci in the visualized ribs, clavicles, scapulae, or thoracic vertebral bodies.

---

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous without focal lesions. No hyper- or hypoattenuating hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder and biliary tract: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. Common bile duct measures 4 mm, within normal limits.

Spleen: Normal size and homogeneous attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal morphology, attenuation, and duct caliber. No peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology without nodularity.

Kidneys and ureters: Bilateral kidneys are normal in size, position, and parenchymal thickness. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi or masses. There is mild bilateral ureteral displacement in the lower abdomen due to the pelvic mass and ascites, without hydroureter or obstructive uropathy.

Gastrointestinal tract: The stomach is unremarkable. Small bowel loops are displaced superiorly and laterally by ascites and pelvic masses but show no wall thickening and no evidence of obstruction. The colon is displaced and partially effaced in the pelvis. The sigmoid colon demonstrates extrinsic mass effect from pelvic implants along the sigmoid mesentery (see below) without luminal narrowing. The appendix is visualized and is not dilated.

Ascites: There is a very large volume of free intraperitoneal fluid distributed throughout the abdomen and pelvis. Fluid is present in the right and left paracolic gutters, the perihepatic and perisplenic spaces (Morison's pouch and left subphrenic space), the mesenteric small bowel compartment, and the entire pelvis. The volume of ascites is estimated at approximately 4 to 5 liters. The fluid is of water attenuation without internal septations or debris on CT, though given the clinical context, malignant ascites is strongly suspected.

Omentum: The greater omentum is markedly thickened and exhibits diffuse heterogeneous soft-tissue infiltration consistent with omental cake — virtually the entire greater omentum from the transverse colon to the pelvis is replaced by confluent lobular soft-tissue attenuation, with loss of the normal fatty architecture. The infiltration is most prominent in the right upper and central abdomen, where the omental thickness measures up to 3.2 cm. A few foci of internal calcification are noted within the omental mass, likely reflecting dystrophic calcification within areas of necrosis. No definitive bowel invasion identified, though the omentum is closely adherent to the transverse colon in the central abdomen.

Peritoneum and peritoneal surfaces:
- Right paracolic gutter: Multiple small soft-tissue nodular implants along the parietal peritoneum, the largest measuring approximately 1.4 cm in short axis. Several additional sub-centimeter nodules are present, consistent with miliary peritoneal seeding.
- Left paracolic gutter: Similar peritoneal nodularity with implants up to 0.9 cm.
- Pelvic peritoneum: Diffuse nodular peritoneal thickening along the cul-de-sac, the vesicouterine fold, and the lateral pelvic walls. Several discrete soft-tissue implants in the rectouterine (Douglas) pouch measuring up to 1.1 cm.
- Sigmoid mesentery: Two distinct soft-tissue implants along the mesenteric leaves of the sigmoid colon, the larger measuring 1.3 cm, producing mild extrinsic mass effect on the sigmoid wall without mucosal invasion.
- Right hemidiaphragm: A small soft-tissue nodule along the inferior surface of the right hemidiaphragm peritoneum, measuring approximately 0.7 cm, consistent with a subdiaphragmatic peritoneal implant. No definitive transdiaphragmatic extension. No right pleural effusion.
- Left hemidiaphragm: No definitive implant. A trace amount of ascites extends into the left subphrenic space.
- Liver capsule / hepatic surface: There is a small peritoneal implant along the anterior hepatic capsule (segment IV/V surface) measuring approximately 0.6 cm; this abuts but does not appear to infiltrate the hepatic parenchyma. No intraparenchymal hepatic lesions.

Adnexal masses:
- Right adnexa: There is a large complex right adnexal mass measuring 9.2 x 7.4 x 8.1 cm (transverse x AP x craniocaudal). The mass is predominantly cystic with numerous irregular thick septations (septum thickness up to 8 mm) and multiple internal papillary projections arising from the septae and wall, some of which demonstrate enhancement. There are areas of internal soft-tissue nodularity with enhancement. The external surface of the mass is irregular and lobulated. The fat plane between the mass and the right pelvic sidewall is partially effaced. The mass is inseparable from the right uterine cornual region. No definitive origin from the uterine body itself is identified.
- Left adnexa: A separate left adnexal mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 x 4.1 cm with morphology similar to the right, demonstrating mixed cystic and solid components, internal papillary excrescences, and thick irregular septations. Enhancing solid components are present. The left adnexal mass is situated posterior to the uterus in the cul-de-sac and abuts the posterior uterine serosa.
- Uterus: The uterus is of normal size and attenuation. The endometrial stripe is not thickened. The uterus is displaced anteriorly and superiorly by the bilateral adnexal masses and ascites.

Lymph nodes:
- Right external iliac chain: An enlarged right external iliac lymph node measuring 2.1 cm in short axis, with loss of the normal fatty hilum. This lymph node is morphologically suspicious for nodal metastasis.
- Left external iliac chain: A few borderline-sized left external iliac nodes measuring up to 1.0 cm in short axis, indeterminate.
- Para-aortic region: A cluster of lymph nodes in the right para-aortic and interaortocaval regions at the level of the L2 vertebral body, with an aggregate cross-sectional diameter of approximately 1.8 cm. Individual nodes measure up to 1.1 cm in short axis. Morphology is suspicious for nodal involvement.
- Left para-aortic: A few left para-aortic nodes measuring up to 0.9 cm, borderline enlarged, indeterminate.
- Pelvic sidewalls: No additional enlarged pelvic nodal groups identified above the iliac vessels (no common iliac adenopathy).
- Inguinal regions: No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially filled and displaced anterosuperiorly by the pelvic masses and ascites. No bladder wall thickening or intraluminal lesion.

Bony pelvis and spine: The visualized osseous structures of the lumbar spine, sacrum, and pelvis are intact without suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions. No vertebral body collapse.

Soft tissues: No soft-tissue masses or lymphadenopathy in the groins. Subcutaneous tissues are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION

1. LARGE BILATERAL COMPLEX ADNEXAL MASSES: A large right adnexal mass (9.2 x 7.4 x 8.1 cm) and a left adnexal mass (4.8 x 3.9 x 4.1 cm), both demonstrating mixed solid-cystic morphology with thick irregular septations, internal papillary projections, and enhancing solid components. This constellation of features is highly suspicious for bilateral primary ovarian or fallopian tube malignancy, most likely high-grade serous carcinoma given the clinical and laboratory context.

2. MASSIVE ASCITES: Very large volume free intraperitoneal fluid distributed throughout the abdomen and pelvis, estimated 4-5 liters. Malignant etiology strongly suspected in this clinical setting.

3. OMENTAL CAKING: Diffuse replacement of the greater omentum by lobular confluent soft-tissue infiltration (omental cake), most prominent in the right upper abdomen with omental thickness up to 3.2 cm. This pattern is characteristic of peritoneal carcinomatosis from an ovarian primary.

4. PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS: Multiple soft-tissue peritoneal implants distributed along the right and left paracolic gutters, the pelvic peritoneum including the rectouterine pouch, the sigmoid mesentery (two discrete implants up to 1.3 cm), the right hemidiaphragm (0.7 cm subdiaphragmatic nodule), and along the anterior hepatic capsular surface (0.6 cm). The distribution is characteristic of advanced peritoneal dissemination from a gynecologic primary.

5. REGIONAL LYMPHADENOPATHY: Enlarged right external iliac lymph node (2.1 cm) and a right para-aortic/interaortocaval nodal cluster (aggregate 1.8 cm) at the L2 level, both morphologically suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement. Borderline left external iliac and left para-aortic nodes are indeterminate.

6. NO DISTANT (M1) DISEASE IDENTIFIED: No hepatic parenchymal metastases. No pulmonary nodules or pulmonary metastases. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No osseous metastases in the visualized skeleton.

Overall, the CT findings are highly characteristic of advanced-stage bilateral ovarian carcinoma with diffuse peritoneal spread, omental cake, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node involvement, and massive malignant ascites. The absence of hepatic parenchymal metastases, pulmonary involvement, or distant disease beyond the peritoneum is consistent with FIGO Stage IIIC disease. Clinical correlation and tissue sampling are advised to establish histologic confirmation.
